1: Ruth is set in the period of the judges ('in the days when the judges ruled', Ruth 1:1) and is a counter-narrative to the chaos of that era: while Judges ends with 'everyone did what was right in his own eyes,' Ruth depicts a community where covenant loyalty (hesed) is practiced across ethnic lines and social classes. The book's theological center is the keyword hesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) — used three times (Ruth 1:8; Ruth 2:20; Ruth 3:10). It serves as an introduction to the Davidic narrative: its genealogy (Ruth 4:17-22) links it directly to 1 Samuel and the rise of David, making Ruth the backstory of the royal family. Matthew's genealogy (Matthew 1:5) includes Ruth alongside Tamar, Rahab, and Bathsheba — four women with irregular stories through whom the messianic line runs.
2: The names carry theological weight: Elimelech (אֱלִימֶלֶךְ) means 'my God is king' — ironic in the age of Judges when there was no king; Naomi (נָעֳמִי) means 'pleasant/sweet,' which she will invert to Mara (v20); Mahlon (מַחְלוֹן) and Chilion (כִּלְיוֹן) likely derive from roots meaning 'sickness/weakness' and 'wasting/destruction' respectively — ominous names that foreshadow their early deaths. Ephrathites from Bethlehem (בֵּית לֶחֶם, 'house of bread') — Ephrathah is the ancestral clan name for the Bethlehem district (Genesis 35:19; Ruth 48:7; Micah 5:2); the family is from Judah's heartland, the region that will eventually produce David and the messianic line. Migration to Moab during famine is archaeologically attested in this region; the Shephelah and Judean hills were vulnerable to drought cycles.
3: Elimelech's death leaves Naomi as a widow in the Iron Age Levant — a position of profound legal and economic vulnerability. Israelite law provided specific protections for widows (ʾalmānāh): gleaning rights (Deuteronomy 24:19-21), exemption from certain pledges (Deuteronomy 24:17), inclusion in tithes (Deuteronomy 26:12-13), and judicial protections (Exodus 22:22-24). But enforcement depended on community goodwill; a widow in a foreign country (Moab) had no such protections at all. The narrative begins with a progressive stripping of Naomi's support system: husband, then (vv4-5) both sons. The sociological realism of the story's opening movements creates the desperate context in which hesed and the go'el institution will operate.
4: Mahlon's and Chilion's marriages to Moabite women are presented without explicit condemnation, though the tension with Deuteronomy 23:3 ('No Ammonite or Moabite may enter the assembly of YHWH...even to the tenth generation') is real. The prohibition in Deuteronomy 23:3 concerns membership in the covenant assembly, not marriage per se; but Israelite tradition (Ezra 9-10; Nehemiah 13:23-25) later treated intermarriage with specifically named nations as a violation. The silence here suggests the narrator is not primarily focused on the legal question but on the theological one: what does it mean when a Moabite woman becomes fully loyal to YHWH and his people? Ruth's subsequent behavior answers the question.
5: The death of both sons leaves three women — two daughters-in-law and Naomi — in a state of triple bereavement. In the ANE world, a woman's identity and economic security were inseparable from male household membership (father, husband, or son). Naomi has lost all three ties. The legal dimension: without a male heir to inherit Elimelech's land in Bethlehem, the family's landholding was at risk of permanent alienation. The go'el institution (Leviticus 25:25) existed precisely for this situation — a near kinsman had the right (and in some circumstances the obligation) to redeem alienated family land and to perpetuate the family name. The entire plot of Ruth turns on whether a go'el will act.
6: The report that 'YHWH had visited his people to give them food' uses the verb pāqaḏ (H6485) — 'to visit, attend to, care for.' The same verb appears in YHWH's promise to Moses: 'I have surely visited you' (Exodus 3:16) and in Jacob's dying words: 'God will surely visit you' (Genesis 50:24-25). The pāqaḏ formula in Scripture signals a turning point in YHWH's providential engagement with his people — an active divine intervention after a period of apparent absence. Naomi hears about this divine visitation while in Moab; the report becomes the occasion of return. The famine and its lifting are both YHWH's sovereign acts (cf. Amos 4:6-8 on famine as divine discipline).
7: The geographical route from Moab to Bethlehem crosses significant terrain: from the Moabite plateau (east of the Dead Sea), down through the Rift Valley, across the Jordan ford near Jericho, and up through the Judean hill country to Bethlehem — a journey of perhaps 50-65 miles with a total elevation change of over 4,000 feet (descending to the Jordan Valley at -1,300 feet below sea level, then ascending to Bethlehem at +2,500 feet). The journey would take several days on foot and was not trivial. The Jordan crossing is also symbolically charged: the entry point into the Promised Land (Josh 3-4), and the route Ruth travels is the reversal of Abraham's journey outward — a Gentile woman entering the land of covenant promise.
8: Naomi's blessing invokes hesed (H2617) — the book's governing keyword. Hesed is often translated 'steadfast love,' 'lovingkindness,' or 'covenant loyalty,' but none fully captures it: it is the loyalty, generosity, and faithfulness that characterizes covenant relationships (between humans and YHWH; between covenant partners). Naomi wishes for Orpah and Ruth the same hesed they showed to 'the dead and to me' — their care for their dead husbands and for her is explicitly characterized as hesed. The term recurs at Ruth 2:20 (Boaz's act is hesed to the living and the dead) and Ruth 3:10 (Ruth's pursuit of Boaz rather than young men is her second act of hesed). The book is fundamentally about hesed in action.
9: Mānôaḥ (H4494, 'resting place' / settled security) is the legal-social concept of a woman finding a safe and stable home within a male-headed household. Naomi had prayed for this for both daughters-in-law (v9); later she will arrange it for Ruth (Ruth 3:1). The term reflects the real vulnerability of unattached women in the Iron Age: economic security, social status, and physical safety all depended on having a place in a functioning household. Mānôaḥ is not merely emotional peace but structural social security — a home, provision, and legal protection. Naomi's inability to provide this for Orpah and Ruth (because she has no sons to offer as husbands) drives the entire plot.
10: Orpah and Ruth's initial response — 'No, we will return with you to your people' — demonstrates genuine hesed. Their willingness to leave their homeland and follow an impoverished old woman to a foreign country is remarkable and is later recognized as such (v8: Naomi blesses them for the hesed they have already shown). The daughters-in-law are not depicted as cowardly or faithless; Orpah makes the reasonable choice Naomi herself recommends. The contrast between Orpah and Ruth is not a contrast between faithfulness and faithlessness but between two kinds of faithfulness: Orpah follows her reasonable obligations, Ruth exceeds them entirely.
11: Naomi's argument is drawn from the levirate marriage principle (Deuteronomy 25:5-10): when a married man dies childless, his brother is obligated to marry the widow and raise up children in the dead brother's name. Naomi inverts the institution: 'If I had a husband tonight and bore sons, would you wait for them?' The argument is deliberately absurd (she is too old) to demonstrate that the levirate path is closed to her daughters-in-law. The same levirate logic underlies the Sadducees' question to Jesus in Matthew 22:23-33 — they use it to argue against resurrection. Jesus's answer that the resurrection age transcends marriage categories is partly an implicit commentary on the temporary nature of the entire levirate system.
12: The hypothetical 'if I have a husband tonight' uses the legal language of concubinage and rapid remarriage that was not uncommon in the ancient Near East. Naomi's argument demonstrates familiarity with the full range of legal mechanisms available to widows; she is not ignorant of her options, she is genuinely without them. The go'el option (which ultimately applies) is not mentioned here — perhaps because Naomi does not know at this point whether any near kinsman remains in Bethlehem, or because she does not think it applies to daughters-in-law rather than blood relatives. The plot's later revelation of Boaz will come as a surprise to her too.
13: 'The hand of YHWH has gone out against me' — Naomi's frank theodicy. The idiom of YHWH's 'hand' going against someone is used throughout the OT for divine judgment or affliction (1 Samuel 5:9; Job 2:10; Psalms 32:4). Naomi does not attribute her suffering to fate, accident, or her own fault; she attributes it directly to YHWH — which is, paradoxically, more theologically coherent than evasion would be. The same frank attribution characterizes Job: 'YHWH gave, and YHWH has taken away; blessed be the name of YHWH' (Job 1:21). The willingness to name YHWH as the author of suffering, rather than seeking a more comfortable explanation, preserves the integrity of faith in a sovereign God while demanding that the faith be faith in that God's ultimate goodness.
14: The text notes both women wept — Orpah's departure is not cold or calculating but genuinely painful. The contrast between 'Orpah kissed her mother-in-law' and 'Ruth clung to her' (using dāḇaq H1692 — the same verb as Genesis 2:24: 'a man shall cleave to his wife'; 2 Kings 18:6: Hezekiah 'clung to YHWH') is stark. Dāḇaq connotes a deep, volitional attachment that overcomes all separation forces. Ruth's clinging to Naomi is the covenant-loyalty act that the rest of the book will validate and reward. Orpah's choice is portrayed as understandable; Ruth's is portrayed as exceeding what was asked or required — and this excess is the book's model of hesed.
15: Naomi's urging Ruth to 'return after your sister-in-law to her people and to her gods' is religiously frank: the Moabite national deity was Chemosh (Numbers 21:29; 1 Kings 11:7; Jeremiah 48:46). The appeal to 'her gods' implicitly names the religious dimension of Orpah's return: to go back to Moab is to go back to the Chemosh cult, to be embedded again in a non-Yahwistic religious community. Ruth's decision to follow Naomi is therefore not merely ethnic but religious — she is choosing YHWH over Chemosh. The contrast between the two gods is part of what Ruth's declaration in v16-17 will make explicit.
16: Ruth's covenant declaration (vv16-17) is one of the most celebrated passages in the Hebrew Bible: 'Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay. Your people shall be my people, and your God my God. Where you die I will die, and there I will be buried. May YHWH deal with me, be it ever so severely, if even death separates you and me.' The declaration operates on multiple levels simultaneously: social (joining Naomi's community), ethnic (becoming part of Israel), religious (adopting YHWH as her God), and legal (invoking YHWH's name in a binding oath). The legal oath formula 'thus may YHWH do to me and thus may he add' (v17) places Ruth under the sanctions of covenant obligation — she is not making a sentimental promise but a binding legal commitment in YHWH's name.
17: The invocation of YHWH by name in the oath — 'YHWH do so to me and more also' — is the standard self-imprecation formula used in binding oaths throughout the historical books (1 Samuel 3:17; Ruth 14:44; 2 Samuel 3:9,35; 1 Kings 2:23). By invoking YHWH's name, Ruth places herself under YHWH's covenant jurisdiction — she is effectively enrolling as a convert to Yahwism, not merely attaching herself to Naomi. The rabbinic tradition later develops a formal conversion process partly on the basis of Ruth's declaration; it becomes the paradigmatic model of what proselyte conversion entails. Ruth's joining of the covenant community is the OT's clearest narrative model of voluntary Gentile adoption into Israel.
18: Naomi's silence when she saw Ruth's resolve (miṣṣaddāh lāleḵet ʾittāh — that she was determined to go with her) is a significant narrative detail. She says nothing more. The silence is not disapproval; it is recognition. A resolution expressed with this level of commitment — a legal oath, a covenant declaration — cannot and should not be argued against. The narrator uses Naomi's silence to signal that Ruth's commitment has the quality of finality. In a culture where verbal exchange is the primary medium of social relation, choosing not to speak a further word of dissuasion is itself a speech act: an acknowledgment that the matter is settled.
19: Bethlehem's response — 'Was the whole city moved/stirred because of them? Is this Naomi?' — uses the verb hōm (H1949: 'to be in commotion, to be in an uproar') which appears elsewhere for the excitement at the arrival of the ark (1 Samuel 4:5) and the commotion of an army camp (1 Samuel 14:15). The village's stir is both social curiosity (a woman returning from long absence with a foreign daughter-in-law) and implicit recognition that something significant has happened. Small Bronze Age and Iron Age villages (Bethlehem was not large) would have known the Elimelech family well; Naomi's return with Ruth but without husband and sons would have been immediately notable.
20: The name-change from Naomi ('pleasant/sweet') to Mara ('bitter') reflects the ANE practice of names as theological commentary on one's condition. Hebrew naming throughout the OT encodes both character and circumstance: Naomi's self-renaming is an act of honest self-interpretation. 'I went out full and YHWH brought me back empty' — ʾel šadday (God Almighty) is the patriarchal name for God (Genesis 17:1; Ruth 28:3; Ruth 35:11) and carries connotations of covenant-making power. Naomi's use of the name is significant: she is appealing to the God who makes and keeps covenant promises — even as she confesses that he has dealt bitterly with her. This is not faithlessness but honest covenant complaint (the pattern of lament psalms).
21: 'I went out full (mĕlēʾāh) and YHWH has brought me back empty (rêqāh)' — the contrast of fullness and emptiness maps onto the agricultural metaphors that will dominate the rest of the book. The harvest season (v22) will reverse the emptiness: Ruth will return from the fields full (Ruth 2:17-18), Naomi will eat Boaz's grain (Ruth 3:15-17), and the book's final image is Naomi nursing Obed, her 'fullness' restored. The reversal is YHWH's answer to the complaint. The verb 'testified against me' (ʿānāh bî) — as if YHWH had appeared as a legal witness against her — is rare and striking; Naomi's language pushes toward the edge of covenant lawsuit rhetoric.
22: The closing of chapter 1 is precisely dated by the harvest calendar: 'at the beginning of the barley harvest' — late March to early April in the Bethlehem area (slightly later than the Jordan Valley). The agricultural calendar of ancient Israel ran: barley harvest (Passover season, Nisan/April) → wheat harvest (Pentecost/Shavuot, Sivan/May-June) → summer figs and grapes (Tammuz-Av/July-August) → olives (Tishri/September-October). Chapter 2's gleaning takes place in the barley harvest; chapter 3's threshing floor scene falls at the end of the barley or wheat harvest; the legal transaction of chapter 4 follows shortly after. The entire story unfolds in the span of approximately two months.
